title: Analog Electronics
description: Analog refresh for EE/radar/software: DC networks through noise, layout and instrumentation. 15 sub-topics, tiered easy to hard.

items.front: Define electric current and give its unit and defining equation.
items.back: Current is the rate of flow of charge: i = dq/dt. Unit is the ampere (A), where 1 A = 1 coulomb per second. Conventional current flows in the direction of positive charge motion (opposite electron flow).
items.hint: Think rate of charge.

items.front: Define voltage (potential difference) and its relation to energy and charge.
items.back: Voltage is energy per unit charge: v = dw/dq. Unit is the volt (V), where 1 V = 1 joule per coulomb. It is the work done moving a unit positive charge between two points.
items.hint: Joules per coulomb.

items.front: State the passive sign convention and what positive power means under it.
items.back: Assign current entering the element at its positive voltage terminal. Then p = v*i. If p > 0 the element absorbs (dissipates) power; if p < 0 it delivers/supplies power. Sources usually supply (negative under PSC), resistors always absorb.
items.hint: Current in at the + terminal.

items.front: Relate power and energy for an electrical element.
items.back: Power is the rate of energy transfer: p = dw/dt, so energy w = integral of p dt. Unit of power is the watt (W = J/s); energy is joules (or watt-hours: 1 Wh = 3600 J).
items.hint: Integrate power over time.

items.front: Distinguish an ideal independent voltage source from an ideal independent current source.
items.back: An ideal voltage source maintains a fixed terminal voltage regardless of current drawn (zero internal resistance). An ideal current source forces a fixed current regardless of terminal voltage (infinite internal resistance). Each delivers whatever the other quantity must be.
items.hint: One fixes V, the other fixes I.

items.front: What is a dependent (controlled) source? Name the four types.
items.back: Its value depends on a voltage or current elsewhere in the circuit. Four types: VCVS (voltage-controlled voltage source), VCCS (voltage-controlled current source), CCVS (current-controlled voltage source), CCCS (current-controlled current source). They model transistors, op-amps, and amplifiers.
items.hint: Two control variables x two output types = 4.

items.prompt: A 12 V battery delivers 2 A to a load for 5 minutes. How much energy is delivered to the load?
items.explanation: P = V*I = 12 V * 2 A = 24 W. Energy = P*t = 24 W * 300 s = 7200 J. The 120 J and 24 J options forget the time or use it wrong; 1440 J uses minutes incorrectly.

items.prompt: Under the passive sign convention, an element has v = +5 V across it and current i = -2 A flowing into its + terminal. The element is:
items.explanation: p = v*i = (5)(-2) = -10 W. Negative power under PSC means the element supplies 10 W to the rest of the circuit.

items.prompt: Which statement about an ideal current source is correct?
choices: It delivers constant current regardless of load voltage
It cannot be left open-circuited or short-circuited
items.explanation: An ideal current source maintains constant current independent of terminal voltage (infinite internal resistance). A short circuit is fine for it; an open circuit is the problematic case (theoretically infinite voltage).

items.prompt: A transconductance amplifier producing an output current proportional to an input voltage is best modeled as which dependent source?
items.explanation: Output current controlled by input voltage = voltage-controlled current source (VCCS). Its gain has units of siemens (transconductance, gm). This is the standard small-signal model element for FETs and BJTs.

items.front: Give resistance in terms of resistivity and geometry, and define conductance.
items.back: R = rho * L / A, where rho is resistivity (ohm*m), L is length, A is cross-sectional area. Conductance G = 1/R, unit siemens (S). Conductivity sigma = 1/rho.
items.hint: Longer and thinner = more R.

items.front: State Ohm's law and the three equivalent power formulas for a resistor.
items.back: Ohm's law: V = I*R. Power dissipated: P = V*I = I^2*R = V^2/R. Use I^2*R when current is known, V^2/R when voltage is known.
items.hint: Substitute Ohm's law into P=VI.

items.front: What does a resistor's temperature coefficient (tempco) specify, and typical units?
items.back: Tempco gives the fractional change in resistance per degree of temperature change, in ppm/degC. R(T) = R0*(1 + alpha*(T - T0)). Metal-film resistors have low tempco (e.g. 25-100 ppm/degC); carbon-composition much higher. Matters for precision and bias stability.
items.hint: ppm per degree C.

items.front: Decode the 4-band resistor color code and the tolerance band.
items.back: Bands 1-2: significant digits; band 3: multiplier (power of 10); band 4: tolerance. Black0 Brown1 Red2 Orange3 Yellow4 Green5 Blue6 Violet7 Grey8 White9. Gold = x0.1 or +/-5%, Silver = x0.01 or +/-10%, no band = +/-20%.
items.hint: BB ROY of Great Britain Very Good Wife.

items.front: State Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).
items.back: KCL: the algebraic sum of currents entering any node is zero (charge conservation). KVL: the algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop is zero (energy conservation). KCL is the basis of nodal analysis; KVL of mesh analysis.
items.hint: One per node, one per loop.

items.front: Why must a resistor's power rating be derated in practice?
items.back: The rating (e.g. 1/4 W) is the max continuous dissipation at a specified ambient temperature with adequate airflow. At higher ambient or poor heat-sinking the safe dissipation drops, so designers run resistors well below rating (often 50%) for reliability and to limit drift/aging.
items.hint: Heat is the limit.

items.prompt: A resistor reads bands Brown, Black, Red, Gold. Its value and tolerance are:
items.explanation: Brown=1, Black=0 gives digits '10'; Red multiplier = x100; so 10*100 = 1000 ohm = 1 kohm. Gold tolerance = +/-5%.

items.prompt: A 100 ohm resistor must dissipate up to 1 W. The maximum continuous voltage you can apply across it is about:
items.explanation: P = V^2/R, so V = sqrt(P*R) = sqrt(1*100) = 10 V. (31.6 V would be sqrt(100*10) and ignores the rating.)

items.prompt: If you double the current through a fixed resistor, the power it dissipates:
items.explanation: P = I^2*R. Power scales with the square of current, so doubling I gives 2^2 = 4 times the power.

items.prompt: At a node, three currents enter: 3 A, -5 A, and an unknown I; one current of 2 A leaves. By KCL, I equals:
items.explanation: Sum of currents into node = 0: 3 + (-5) + I - 2 = 0, so I - 4 = 0, I = 4 A. (Treat the 2 A leaving as -2 A entering.)

items.front: How do resistances combine in series and in parallel?
items.back: Series: R_eq = R1 + R2 + ... (same current). Parallel: 1/R_eq = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... (same voltage); for two, R_eq = R1*R2/(R1+R2). Parallel equivalent is always smaller than the smallest branch.
items.hint: Conductances add in parallel.

items.front: State the voltage divider rule (memorize).
items.back: For resistors in series sharing source V, the voltage across Rk is Vk = V * Rk / (sum of series resistances). For two: V_out = V_in * R2/(R1+R2). Valid only when negligible current is drawn from the tap (else loading).
items.hint: Across = its own R over total R.

items.front: State the current divider rule for two parallel resistors (memorize).
items.back: Total current I splits inversely with resistance: I1 = I * R2/(R1+R2), I2 = I * R1/(R1+R2). The larger current goes through the smaller resistor. In conductance form, Ik = I * Gk/G_total.
items.hint: Each branch gets the OTHER resistor over total.

items.front: Describe the Wheatstone bridge and its balance condition.
items.back: Two voltage dividers (R1/R2 and R3/R4) share one source; the bridge output is the difference between the two midpoints. Balanced (zero output) when R1/R2 = R3/R4, i.e. R1*R4 = R2*R3. Used to measure unknown R precisely and in sensor (strain-gauge) front ends.
items.hint: Ratios of the two arms equal.

items.front: When do you need a Delta-Wye (pi-T) transformation, and give the Delta-to-Wye formula.
items.back: When resistors are neither in series nor parallel (e.g. a bridge that isn't balanced), convert to simplify. Delta (Rab, Rbc, Rca) to Wye: each Wye resistor = product of the two adjacent Delta resistors / sum of all three Delta resistors. E.g. Ra = Rab*Rca/(Rab+Rbc+Rca).
items.hint: Wye leg = product of adjacent / sum of all.

items.front: Why does a resistive voltage divider 'sag' under load, and how does this connect to amplifier design?
items.back: Connecting a load R_L in parallel with the lower divider resistor reduces effective resistance, lowering V_out below the unloaded value. Rule of thumb: make load (input) impedance >> divider impedance. This is the loading concept behind impedance matching and the need for high input impedance / low output impedance in amplifier stages.
items.hint: Load in parallel lowers the tap voltage.

items.prompt: Two resistors, 6 ohm and 3 ohm, are in parallel. The equivalent resistance is:
items.explanation: R_eq = R1*R2/(R1+R2) = (6*3)/(6+3) = 18/9 = 2 ohm. Parallel equivalent is smaller than the smaller branch (3 ohm), confirming 2 ohm.

items.prompt: A 10 V source feeds R1 = 4 kohm in series with R2 = 1 kohm. With no load, V across R2 is:
items.explanation: Voltage divider: V_R2 = V*R2/(R1+R2) = 10*1k/(4k+1k) = 10*(1/5) = 2 V.

items.prompt: A current source of 9 A feeds two parallel resistors, 2 ohm and 1 ohm. The current through the 2 ohm resistor is:
items.explanation: Current divider: I_2ohm = I * (other R)/(sum) = 9 * 1/(2+1) = 3 A. The smaller (1 ohm) resistor carries the larger 6 A, as expected.

items.prompt: A Wheatstone bridge has R1 = 100 ohm, R2 = 200 ohm, R3 = 150 ohm. For balance, R4 must be:
items.explanation: Balance: R1/R2 = R3/R4, so R4 = R3*R2/R1 = 150*200/100 = 300 ohm. Equivalent check: R1*R4 = R2*R3 gives 100*300 = 200*150 = 30000.

items.front: Nodal vs mesh analysis: when is each less work?
items.back: Nodal (KCL, node voltages) is leaner when there are fewer nodes than meshes and many current sources / parallel branches. Mesh (KVL, loop currents) is leaner with fewer meshes than nodes and many voltage sources / series branches. Count unknowns: choose the method with fewer equations. Mesh requires a planar circuit.
items.hint: Compare (nodes-1) vs (meshes).

items.front: State the superposition theorem and its key restriction.
items.back: In a linear circuit, the response (voltage or current) is the algebraic sum of responses caused by each independent source acting alone. To suppress others: replace independent voltage sources with shorts, independent current sources with opens; leave dependent sources active. Does NOT apply to power (nonlinear in V/I).
items.hint: One independent source at a time; not for power.

items.front: Define the Thevenin equivalent and how to find V_th and R_th.
items.back: Any linear two-terminal network reduces to V_th in series with R_th. V_th = open-circuit voltage at the terminals. R_th = resistance seen from the terminals with independent sources deactivated (voltage->short, current->open). With dependent sources, apply a test source and compute R_th = v_test/i_test.
items.hint: Open-circuit V and looking-back R.

items.front: Define the Norton equivalent and relate it to Thevenin.
items.back: Norton: a current source I_N in parallel with R_N. I_N = short-circuit current at the terminals, R_N = R_th. Relation: V_th = I_N * R_th and I_N = V_th / R_th. Source transformation converts freely between the two.
items.hint: Short-circuit current, same R as Thevenin.

items.front: State the source transformation between a real voltage source and a real current source.
items.back: A voltage source V_s in series with R is equivalent (at its terminals) to a current source I_s = V_s/R in parallel with the same R, and vice versa. The resistor value stays the same; only the topology and source type change.
items.hint: I_s = V_s/R, same R, series<->parallel.

items.front: State the maximum power transfer theorem for DC and the power delivered at the match.
items.back: A source with Thevenin resistance R_th delivers maximum power to a load when R_load = R_th. At the match, P_max = V_th^2 / (4*R_th), and efficiency is only 50% (half the power is lost in R_th). This is the DC analog of conjugate impedance matching in RF/amplifier design.
items.hint: R_load = R_th; half the voltage drops internally.

items.front: How does a real voltmeter's internal resistance cause measurement (loading) error?
items.back: A voltmeter has large but finite input resistance; placed in parallel it draws some current, slightly lowering the measured node voltage. Error is worst across high-impedance nodes. An ammeter (small but nonzero series R) similarly adds burden voltage. Modern DMMs have ~10 Mohm input to minimize loading.
items.hint: Meter in parallel = extra load.

items.prompt: A network has a Thevenin voltage of 12 V and R_th = 6 ohm. The maximum power deliverable to a load is:
items.explanation: Max power at R_load = R_th: P_max = V_th^2/(4*R_th) = 144/(4*6) = 144/24 = 6 W.

items.prompt: To find R_th of a network containing only independent sources and resistors, you should:
choices: Leave all sources active and measure terminal voltage
Short voltage sources, open current sources, then find terminal resistance
Open voltage sources, short current sources, then find terminal resistance
Apply a test current and divide by the open-circuit voltage
items.explanation: Deactivate independent sources by their internal resistance: ideal voltage source -> short (0 ohm), ideal current source -> open (infinite ohm). Then compute the resistance looking into the terminals. The test-source method is only required when dependent sources are present.

items.prompt: A real 9 V battery with 1.5 ohm internal resistance is shorted by an ideal ammeter. The current reads approximately:
items.explanation: The internal resistance limits short-circuit current: I = V/R_int = 9/1.5 = 6 A. An ideal source would give infinite current, but real internal resistance bounds it (this is also the Norton short-circuit current I_N).

items.prompt: Why can superposition NOT be used to directly add up the power contributions of individual sources?
choices: Power depends on V^2 or I^2, a nonlinear function of the responses
Superposition only works for AC circuits
Dependent sources must be turned off
items.explanation: Superposition applies to linear quantities (the V and I responses add). Power involves products/squares (P = I^2*R), which is nonlinear, so the squared sum of currents is not the sum of squared currents. Compute total V or I first, then power.

items.prompt: You measure 5.00 V across a 1 Mohm node using a DMM with 10 Mohm input resistance. The true (unloaded) node voltage was closest to:
items.explanation: The meter loads the node. If the source were a divider with 1 Mohm Thevenin resistance, the meter's 10 Mohm reads V_true * 10/(10+1) = 0.909*V_true. So V_true = 5.00/0.909 = 5.50 V. Loading pulled the reading down about 9%.

items.front: What charge does a capacitor store for a given voltage?
items.back: Q = C*V. Charge is linearly proportional to the applied voltage, with capacitance C as the constant of proportionality (units: coulombs = farads * volts).
items.hint: Charge, capacitance, voltage relationship.

items.front: Write the constitutive (i-v) relation for an ideal capacitor.
items.back: i = C*dv/dt. The current through a capacitor is proportional to the RATE OF CHANGE of voltage across it, not the voltage itself.
items.hint: Current depends on how fast voltage changes.

items.front: How much energy is stored in a charged capacitor?
items.back: E = 0.5*C*V^2. Energy is stored in the electric field between the plates and scales with the square of voltage.
items.hint: Half C V squared.

items.front: Why can the voltage across a capacitor not change instantaneously?
items.back: Because i = C*dv/dt, an instantaneous voltage step (dv/dt -> infinity) would require infinite current. With finite current, voltage must change continuously. This is why caps smooth/hold voltage.
items.hint: Look at the i-v relation and what an infinite slope implies.

items.front: How does an ideal capacitor respond to DC versus AC in steady state?
items.back: It blocks DC (passes no steady current once charged, acts like an open) and passes AC (the higher the frequency, the lower its impedance magnitude).
items.hint: Open at DC, conductive at high frequency.

items.prompt: A capacitor carries i = C*dv/dt. If the voltage across a 10uF cap rises linearly at 2 V/ms, what current flows?
items.explanation: i = C*dv/dt = 10e-6 F * (2 V / 1e-3 s) = 10e-6 * 2000 = 20e-3 A = 20 mA. A constant dv/dt gives a constant current.

items.prompt: In DC steady state, what does an ideal capacitor look like to the circuit?
items.explanation: At DC, dv/dt = 0 so i = 0 regardless of voltage. No current flows, which is the behavior of an open circuit. (At very high frequency it instead approaches a short.)

items.prompt: You double the voltage on a capacitor. By what factor does the stored energy change?
items.explanation: E = 0.5*C*V^2 scales with V squared, so doubling V multiplies energy by 2^2 = 4.

items.prompt: Which statement about Q = C*V is correct for a fixed (linear) capacitor?
choices: Doubling V halves the stored charge
Charge depends on dv/dt, not V
items.explanation: For a linear capacitor, charge is directly proportional to voltage with constant C: Q = C*V. The dv/dt relation governs current, not stored charge.

items.front: Write the constitutive (i-v) relation for an ideal inductor.
items.back: v = L*di/dt. The voltage across an inductor is proportional to the RATE OF CHANGE of current through it. This is the dual of the capacitor's i = C*dv/dt.
items.hint: Voltage depends on how fast current changes.

items.front: How much energy is stored in an inductor carrying current I?
items.back: E = 0.5*L*I^2. Energy is stored in the magnetic field and scales with the square of current (dual of the capacitor's 0.5*C*V^2).
items.hint: Half L I squared.

items.front: Why can the current through an inductor not change instantaneously?
items.back: Because v = L*di/dt, an instantaneous current step (di/dt -> infinity) would require infinite voltage. Inductors resist sudden current changes; interrupting inductor current causes large voltage spikes (flyback).
items.hint: Infinite slope of current implies infinite voltage.

items.front: How does an ideal inductor respond to DC versus AC in steady state?
items.back: It passes DC (acts like a short / wire once current settles, di/dt = 0 so v = 0) and blocks AC (the higher the frequency, the higher its impedance magnitude). Opposite of a capacitor.
items.hint: Short at DC, blocking at high frequency.

items.front: What is DCR in a real inductor?
items.back: DCR = DC resistance: the resistance of the winding wire itself. It causes I^2*R loss, limits current handling, and adds a real (resistive) term in series with the ideal inductance. Lower DCR = higher efficiency in power inductors.
items.hint: Resistance of the copper, not the reactance.

items.front: What is core saturation in an inductor, and why does it matter?
items.back: Above a certain current the magnetic core's domains fully align; permeability collapses and effective inductance drops sharply. The inductor no longer limits di/dt, current can run away. Critical in switching power supplies; pick parts with adequate saturation current rating.
items.hint: Magnetic material runs out of room; inductance falls off.

items.prompt: A 100 mH inductor has current rising at 50 mA/ms. What voltage appears across it?
items.explanation: v = L*di/dt = 0.1 H * (50e-3 A / 1e-3 s) = 0.1 * 50 = 5 V. A constant di/dt gives a constant voltage.

items.prompt: In DC steady state, what does an ideal inductor look like to the circuit?
items.explanation: At DC, di/dt = 0 so v = 0 across an ideal inductor regardless of current: it behaves as a short. A real inductor still shows its DCR. This is the opposite of a capacitor, which is open at DC.

items.prompt: Why does abruptly opening a switch in series with a current-carrying inductor often produce an arc or large spike?
choices: The inductor stores charge that dumps out
Forcing di/dt large and negative makes v = L*di/dt very large
The capacitance of the wire resonates
DCR increases when the switch opens
items.explanation: Opening the switch tries to force current to zero almost instantly (large negative di/dt). Since v = L*di/dt, the inductor develops a large voltage to oppose the change, which can arc the contacts. This is why flyback diodes/snubbers are used.

items.front: What does the j-operator represent physically in AC circuit analysis?
items.back: j = sqrt(-1) represents a 90 degree counterclockwise rotation (phase shift) in the complex plane. Multiplying a phasor by j advances its phase by +90 degrees. It encodes the time-derivative relationship between v and i in reactive elements.
items.hint: Think rotation, not just an imaginary number.

items.front: Give the complex impedance of a capacitor, Z_C.
items.back: Z_C = 1/(j*w*C) = -j/(w*C), where w = 2*pi*f. Magnitude |Z_C| = 1/(w*C) falls as frequency rises. The -j means current leads voltage by 90 degrees.
items.hint: One over j omega C.

items.front: Give the complex impedance of an inductor, Z_L.
items.back: Z_L = j*w*L, where w = 2*pi*f. Magnitude |Z_L| = w*L rises as frequency rises. The +j means voltage leads current by 90 degrees.
items.hint: j omega L.

items.front: What is the difference between reactance and resistance?
items.back: Resistance (R, real part) dissipates power and is frequency-independent. Reactance (X, imaginary part of Z) stores and returns energy each cycle, dissipates no average power, and is frequency-dependent. Z = R + jX. Inductive X > 0, capacitive X < 0.
items.hint: Real dissipates; imaginary stores.

items.front: What is admittance Y, and how does it relate to impedance?
items.back: Y = 1/Z = G + jB, where G is conductance and B is susceptance (units: siemens). Admittance is convenient for parallel circuits since admittances add in parallel, just as impedances add in series.
items.hint: Reciprocal of impedance; the parallel-friendly form.

items.front: As frequency increases, which way does |Z| move for a capacitor versus an inductor?
items.back: Capacitor: |Z_C| = 1/(w*C) DECREASES with frequency (open at DC, short at high f). Inductor: |Z_L| = w*L INCREASES with frequency (short at DC, open at high f). They are duals.
items.hint: Cap goes down, inductor goes up.

items.prompt: What is the impedance magnitude of a 1 uF capacitor at 1 kHz? (w = 2*pi*f)
items.explanation: |Z_C| = 1/(w*C) = 1/(2*pi*1000*1e-6) = 1/(6.283e-3) = 159 ohms. Quick check: it should fall by 10x at 10 kHz.

items.prompt: For an ideal inductor, the impedance is Z_L = j*w*L. What does the factor of j tell you about phase?
choices: Voltage and current are in phase
items.explanation: The +j is a +90 degree rotation applied to the current phasor to get voltage, so the inductor voltage leads its current by 90 degrees (ELI: E leads I in an L). For a capacitor (-j), current leads voltage (ICE).

items.prompt: An impedance is measured as Z = 50 - j80 ohms at some frequency. What is its character?
items.explanation: The reactance X = -80 ohms is negative, indicating net capacitive behavior. A positive imaginary part would be inductive. The 50 ohm real part is the resistive component.

items.prompt: Why does reactance dissipate no average power while resistance does?
choices: Reactive current and voltage are 90 degrees out of phase, so average power is zero
Reactance is always zero in real parts
items.explanation: Average power is V*I*cos(phase). For a pure reactance the v-i phase difference is 90 degrees and cos(90) = 0, so energy is alternately stored and returned with no net dissipation. Resistance has 0 degree phase, cos(0) = 1, so it dissipates.

lessons.title: Real Parts: ESR/ESL, Self-Resonance, Mutual Inductance & Transformers

items.front: What are ESR and ESL in a real capacitor?
items.back: ESR = equivalent series resistance (lead/plate/dielectric losses), causes heating and ripple voltage and sets the minimum impedance. ESL = equivalent series inductance (leads, internal geometry). A real cap is modeled as C in series with ESR and ESL.
items.hint: One resistive parasitic, one inductive parasitic.

items.front: Why does a 1 uF capacitor look inductive above its self-resonant frequency (SRF)?
items.back: A real cap is C in series with ESL. Below SRF the 1/(w*C) capacitive term dominates (|Z| falls). At SRF they cancel and |Z| = ESR (minimum). Above SRF the w*ESL inductive term dominates, so |Z| rises with frequency: it behaves like an inductor.
items.hint: ESL takes over once 1/(wC) shrinks below w*ESL.

items.front: What is an inductor's self-resonant frequency (SRF)?
items.back: SRF is where the inductor's winding capacitance resonates with L. Below SRF it acts inductive (|Z| rises); above SRF the parasitic capacitance dominates and it acts capacitive (|Z| falls). Use inductors well below their SRF.
items.hint: Parasitic turn-to-turn capacitance resonates with L.

items.front: What is DC-bias derating in Class II (e.g. X7R, X5R) ceramic capacitors?
items.back: In Class II ceramics the high-k dielectric loses permeability/permittivity under applied DC voltage, so effective capacitance drops sharply with bias (often 50%+ at rated voltage). Class I (C0G/NP0) caps do not derate this way but have lower density.
items.hint: High-k MLCCs lose capacitance under DC voltage.

items.front: Compare ceramic Class I (C0G/NP0), film, electrolytic, tantalum, and supercap by typical use.
items.back: C0G/NP0: small, ultra-stable, low loss, timing/RF. Class II (X7R etc.): high density, decoupling, derates with bias/temp. Film: stable, low ESR, audio/snubber/high-voltage. Electrolytic: large bulk capacitance, higher ESR, bulk filtering. Tantalum: stable bulk in small size, watch failure mode. Supercap: farads for energy/backup, very low voltage.
items.hint: Stability vs density vs bulk vs energy.

items.front: State the ideal transformer turns-ratio relations for voltage and current.
items.back: With turns ratio n = N_primary/N_secondary: V_p/V_s = n and I_p/I_s = 1/n. Voltage scales with turns, current inversely, so power is conserved (V_p*I_p = V_s*I_s for the ideal case).
items.hint: Volts up means amps down by the same ratio.

items.front: How does a transformer reflect (transform) impedance, and why is that useful for matching?
items.back: A load Z_L on the secondary appears on the primary as Z_in = n^2 * Z_L, where n = N_p/N_s. By choosing the turns ratio you can match a source to a load (e.g. match a low-Z speaker or antenna to a high-Z source) for maximum power transfer.
items.hint: Impedance scales by the turns ratio squared.

items.front: What does the dot convention indicate on coupled inductors / transformer windings?
items.back: The dots mark terminals with the same instantaneous voltage polarity: current entering the dotted terminal of one winding induces a voltage that is positive at the dotted terminal of the other. It tells you the relative phase (in-phase vs inverted) of the windings.
items.hint: Same-polarity terminals; sets winding phase.

items.prompt: For low-impedance power decoupling near an IC, why is a small-value ceramic (e.g. 0.1 uF) placed alongside a larger bulk capacitor?
choices: The small cap has higher capacitance
The small cap has a higher self-resonant frequency, so it keeps impedance low where the bulk cap has gone inductive
The bulk cap blocks DC and the small one passes it
Small caps have higher ESR which helps
items.explanation: Each cap is low-impedance only up to its SRF, above which ESL makes it inductive. A small cap has lower ESL/C and thus a higher SRF, covering high frequencies where the bulk cap is already inductive. Combining values broadens the low-impedance band (with care about anti-resonance).

items.prompt: An 8 ohm speaker must be matched to an amplifier that wants to see 800 ohms. What transformer turns ratio (primary:secondary) is needed?
items.explanation: Z_in = n^2 * Z_L, so n^2 = 800/8 = 100, giving n = 10. Primary has 10x the turns of the secondary (10:1), stepping the 8 ohm load up to 800 ohms at the primary.

items.prompt: At a capacitor's self-resonant frequency, what is its impedance approximately equal to?
items.explanation: At SRF the capacitive reactance 1/(w*C) and inductive reactance w*ESL cancel, leaving only the real part: |Z| equals the ESR. This is the minimum-impedance point and is why low ESR matters for decoupling.

items.prompt: You design a 5V rail decoupling network using an X7R MLCC rated 16V. At the operating bias, you measure far less capacitance than the marked value. Most likely cause?
choices: DC-bias derating of the Class II dielectric
items.explanation: Class II (X7R/X5R) ceramics lose effective capacitance under applied DC bias; even well below the voltage rating you can lose a large fraction. Always check the manufacturer's capacitance-vs-bias curve, or use C0G/film where stability matters.

items.front: Write the general time-domain sinusoid and name its four descriptors.
items.back: v(t) = Vm*cos(w*t + phi). Vm = amplitude (peak), w = angular frequency (rad/s), phi = phase (rad or deg), and f = w/(2*pi) = ordinary frequency (Hz).
items.hint: Amplitude, angular frequency, phase; frequency is derived from w.

items.front: Relate angular frequency w, frequency f, and period T.
items.back: w = 2*pi*f and f = 1/T, so w = 2*pi/T. Example: 60 Hz gives w = 2*pi*60 = 377 rad/s.
items.hint: w is in rad/s; f is in Hz; T is in seconds.

items.front: What is a phasor, and how does it relate to a time-domain sinusoid?
items.back: A phasor is a complex number encoding amplitude and phase of a single-frequency sinusoid: V = Vm angle(phi). It comes from v(t) = Re{Vm*e^(j*phi) * e^(j*w*t)}. The phasor V = Vm*e^(j*phi) is the constant complex amplitude; e^(j*w*t) (the rotation) is suppressed because it is common to every signal at that frequency.
items.hint: Strip off the common e^(j*w*t); keep the complex amplitude.

items.front: Why do phasors work only for linear, single-frequency, steady-state analysis?
items.back: Linearity lets us use superposition and the complex exponential as an eigenfunction of LTI systems (derivatives/integrals just scale by j*w or 1/(j*w)). Single-frequency keeps one common e^(j*w*t) to factor out. Steady-state means transients have died, so only the forced sinusoidal response remains. Nonlinear elements or multiple frequencies break the single-phasor representation.
items.hint: Eigenfunction of LTI systems; common rotation factor; transients gone.

items.prompt: A signal is v(t) = 10*cos(377*t + 30 deg) V. What is its phasor (peak convention)?
items.explanation: In the peak convention the phasor magnitude equals the amplitude (10) and the angle equals the phase inside the cosine (+30 deg). w = 377 rad/s is the suppressed frequency, not part of the phasor. 7.07 would be the RMS-convention magnitude.

items.prompt: To convert a sine into cosine-reference phasor form, which identity is correct?
items.explanation: sin(x) lags cos(x) by 90 deg, so sin(x) = cos(x - 90 deg). Thus v(t)=5*sin(w*t) has phasor 5 angle -90 deg. Always convert to a single cosine reference before reading off the phase.

items.prompt: Two 60 Hz signals: A = cos(w*t) and B = cos(w*t - 45 deg). The phase relationship is:
choices: A leads B by 315 deg only
items.explanation: B has a more negative phase (-45 deg vs 0), so B reaches its peak later in time; A leads B by 45 deg. Leading means peaking earlier (more positive phase angle).

items.front: How does differentiation in time map to the phasor domain, and why is that useful?
items.back: d/dt becomes multiplication by j*w; integration becomes division by j*w. This turns the differential equations of RLC circuits into algebraic equations in the phasor domain, which is the entire payoff of phasor analysis.
items.hint: Calculus becomes algebra: multiply or divide by j*w.

items.prompt: Why can a Radar tech think of a phasor as a 'snapshot' of a rotating vector?
choices: The vector rotates at w rad/s; the phasor freezes it at t=0, keeping only length and starting angle
The phasor stores the instantaneous voltage at every time
The phasor rotates faster than the real signal
The phasor discards amplitude and keeps only frequency
items.explanation: The full representation Vm*e^(j*(w*t+phi)) is a vector of length Vm spinning at w. Since w is common to all signals, we freeze the rotation and keep the t=0 snapshot: length Vm and angle phi. Projecting the spinning vector onto the real axis reconstructs v(t).

items.front: Give the impedance of R, L, and C in the phasor domain.
items.back: Z_R = R (real). Z_L = j*w*L (purely positive reactance, voltage leads current by 90 deg). Z_C = 1/(j*w*C) = -j/(w*C) (negative reactance, current leads voltage by 90 deg).
items.hint: Resistor real; inductor +j*w*L; capacitor -j/(w*C).

items.front: Define impedance Z and admittance Y, including their parts.
items.back: Z = V/I = R + j*X (ohms), where R is resistance and X is reactance. Y = 1/Z = G + j*B (siemens), where G is conductance and B is susceptance. Note G is generally NOT 1/R unless the element is purely resistive.
items.hint: Z = R + jX; Y = G + jB; Y = 1/Z.

items.prompt: At w = 1000 rad/s, a 10 uF capacitor has impedance approximately:
items.explanation: Z_C = -j/(w*C) = -j/(1000 * 10e-6) = -j/(0.01) = -j*100 ohm. The reactance is negative (capacitive) and current leads voltage by 90 deg.

items.prompt: Series R and L: R = 30 ohm, w*L = 40 ohm. The magnitude and phase of Z are:
items.explanation: Z = 30 + j40. |Z| = sqrt(30^2 + 40^2) = 50. Angle = atan2(40,30) = +53.1 deg. Positive angle is inductive (voltage leads current).

items.front: Do KVL, KCL, series/parallel rules, and voltage/current dividers hold in the phasor domain?
items.back: Yes. All linear network laws apply unchanged using complex phasor voltages/currents and complex impedances. Series Z add; parallel Z combine as 1/Z_total = sum(1/Z_i) or use products-over-sums. Dividers use impedance ratios. The only change is you do complex arithmetic instead of real.
items.hint: Same laws, complex numbers instead of reals.

items.front: How do Thevenin equivalents and superposition work in the phasor domain?
items.back: Thevenin: V_th is the open-circuit phasor voltage, Z_th is the equivalent complex impedance seen from the terminals with independent sources zeroed (voltage sources shorted, current sources opened). Superposition: sum the phasor responses from each source. Caveat: superposition across sources of DIFFERENT frequencies must be summed in the time domain, since phasors at different w cannot be added directly.
items.hint: V_th, Z_th complex; different frequencies must combine in time domain.

items.prompt: In phasor nodal analysis, the conductance matrix entries become:
items.explanation: Nodal analysis is built on KCL with admittances. In the phasor domain each branch contributes a complex admittance Y = 1/Z to the Y-matrix, so [Y][V] = [I] is solved with complex arithmetic.

items.prompt: What happens to a series RLC impedance at the resonant frequency w0 = 1/sqrt(L*C)?
choices: The reactances cancel and Z = R (purely resistive, minimum magnitude)
items.explanation: At w0, j*w*L and -j/(w*C) are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign, so they cancel. The series impedance reduces to just R, giving minimum |Z| and unity power factor (current in phase with voltage).

items.front: Why does an inductor's voltage lead its current by 90 deg, in phasor terms?
items.back: V_L = (j*w*L)*I. Multiplying by j adds +90 deg to the current's angle, so the voltage phasor sits 90 deg ahead of the current phasor. Memory aid ELI: in an inductor (L), voltage (E) leads current (I).
items.hint: Multiply by j = rotate +90 deg. ELI the ICE man.

items.front: Define RMS value and give it for a sinusoid. Why is RMS the standard?
items.back: RMS = sqrt(mean of v(t)^2 over a period). For a sinusoid Vrms = Vm/sqrt(2) = 0.707*Vm. RMS is used because a DC source of that value delivers the same average power to a resistor as the AC signal; it makes P = Vrms^2/R work identically to DC.
items.hint: Root-mean-square; Vm/sqrt(2); equal heating power as DC.

items.front: Write real power P, reactive power Q, and apparent power S for an AC load with angle phi.
items.back: P = Vrms*Irms*cos(phi) watts (W) = average power actually consumed. Q = Vrms*Irms*sin(phi) volt-amps reactive (VAR) = energy shuttled to/from reactive elements. S = Vrms*Irms volt-amps (VA) = |complex power|. Relationship: S^2 = P^2 + Q^2, and complex power S_complex = P + jQ.
items.hint: cos for P, sin for Q, product for S; phi is V-minus-I angle.

items.front: Define power factor and the meaning of leading vs lagging.
items.back: Power factor PF = cos(phi) = P/S, where phi is the angle by which voltage leads current. Lagging PF: inductive load, current lags voltage (phi > 0), Q positive. Leading PF: capacitive load, current leads voltage (phi < 0), Q negative. PF = 1 means purely resistive.
items.hint: cos(phi) = P/S; inductive = lagging, capacitive = leading.

items.prompt: A load draws 120 Vrms and 5 Arms with current lagging voltage by 60 deg. Real power P is:
items.explanation: P = Vrms*Irms*cos(phi) = 120 * 5 * cos(60 deg) = 600 * 0.5 = 300 W. S = 600 VA and Q = 600*sin(60) = 519.6 VAR; only the cos(phi) component is real power.

items.prompt: On the power triangle, the angle between the P axis and the hypotenuse S equals:
items.explanation: The triangle has P (horizontal), Q (vertical), and S (hypotenuse). tan(phi) = Q/P, cos(phi) = P/S, so the angle is phi, the same angle as the load impedance and the power factor angle.

items.prompt: Why do utilities penalize a low (lagging) power factor?
choices: High reactive current raises Irms and I^2*R line losses without delivering real power
Low PF means the load consumes more real power
Low PF reduces apparent power below real power
items.explanation: At low PF, S is large relative to P, so Irms = S/Vrms is high. That extra reactive current heats the distribution wiring (I^2*R losses) and uses transformer/line capacity while doing no useful work, hence PF-correction capacitors are added.

items.prompt: Complex power is most directly computed from phasors as:
choices: S = V * conj(I), using RMS phasors
items.explanation: S = V * I_conjugate (with RMS phasors) yields P + jQ directly. Conjugating I makes the resulting angle equal to phi = (angle of V) - (angle of I), so Re{S} = P and Im{S} = Q come out correctly.

items.front: Conceptually, what is a balanced three-phase system and why is it used?
items.back: Three sources of equal magnitude separated by 120 deg (a, b, c). Benefits: instantaneous total power is constant (no 120 Hz pulsation as in single-phase), more power per conductor mass, and self-canceling neutral current when balanced. Configurations: wye (Y, has neutral) and delta. Line-to-line voltage = sqrt(3) times phase voltage in a wye.
items.hint: Three 120-deg-spaced sources; constant power; wye vs delta; sqrt(3) factor.

items.prompt: In a balanced wye system with phase (line-to-neutral) voltage 120 Vrms, the line-to-line voltage is about:
items.explanation: Line-to-line = sqrt(3) * phase voltage = 1.732 * 120 = 207.8, about 208 Vrms. This is the familiar 120/208 V wye distribution. The sqrt(3) comes from the 120-deg phasor subtraction of two phase voltages.

items.front: Define the transfer function H(j*w) and its two plotted quantities.
items.back: H(j*w) = Y_phasor / X_phasor (output over input) as a function of frequency. It is complex: magnitude |H(j*w)| (gain) and phase angle(H(j*w)) (phase shift). A Bode plot graphs gain in dB and phase in degrees, both versus log frequency.
items.hint: Complex ratio of output to input phasor; magnitude and phase vs log w.

items.front: Define a decade and the dB used in Bode magnitude plots.
items.back: A decade is a 10x change in frequency (e.g., 100 to 1000 rad/s). Magnitude in dB = 20*log10(|H|). The x-axis is logarithmic in frequency; the magnitude y-axis is in dB. Note: 20*log10 is used for voltage/amplitude ratios; 10*log10 is for power ratios.
items.hint: Decade = factor of 10 in w; dB = 20*log10(|H|).

items.front: State the asymptotic magnitude-slope rules for a single real pole and a single real zero.
items.back: A pole at w_p: magnitude flat below w_p, then slopes DOWN at -20 dB/decade above it. A zero at w_z: flat below w_z, then slopes UP at +20 dB/decade above it. Slopes add: two poles give -40 dB/decade. At the corner the actual curve is 3 dB off the asymptote (down for a pole, up for a zero).
items.hint: Pole = -20 dB/dec, zero = +20 dB/dec, slopes superpose, 3 dB at corner.

items.front: Describe the phase asymptotes of a single real pole on a Bode phase plot.
items.back: A real pole contributes 0 deg well below the corner, -45 deg AT the corner w_p, and -90 deg well above it. The transition is roughly linear over a decade below to a decade above the corner (w_p/10 to 10*w_p). A real zero is the mirror image: 0 to +45 to +90 deg.
items.hint: Pole phase: 0 -> -45 (at corner) -> -90, spread over two decades.

items.prompt: For a first-order low-pass H(j*w) = 1/(1 + j*w/w_c), what is |H| in dB at the corner w = w_c?
items.explanation: At w = w_c, H = 1/(1+j) so |H| = 1/sqrt(2) = 0.707. In dB: 20*log10(0.707) = -3 dB. This is the half-power point; the asymptotes (0 dB flat and -20 dB/dec) intersect here while the real curve is 3 dB below.

items.prompt: A measured magnitude response is flat, then rolls off at -40 dB/decade past 1 kHz. The most likely cause is:
choices: Two poles near 1 kHz (or a double pole)
One pole and one zero at 1 kHz
items.explanation: Each real pole adds -20 dB/decade. A -40 dB/decade slope requires two poles contributing in that region (or a repeated pole). One pole alone gives -20; a zero adds +20; a pole-zero pair near the same frequency would cancel to roughly flat.

items.prompt: Fast Bode sketch of H(j*w) = 10 / (1 + j*w/100). The low-frequency gain asymptote is:
choices: +20 dB, flat until the corner at w = 100
items.explanation: At low w the term j*w/100 is negligible, so H approaches 10. 20*log10(10) = +20 dB, flat. The pole at w_c = 100 then bends the curve down at -20 dB/decade. Phase goes 0 -> -45 deg (at w=100) -> -90 deg.

items.prompt: On a Bode magnitude plot, a constant gain of 100 (a flat scalar) appears as:
items.explanation: 20*log10(100) = 20*2 = 40 dB. A pure gain has no frequency dependence, so it is a horizontal line at +40 dB and contributes 0 deg of phase.

items.prompt: You read a Bode plot: flat at +20 dB to 1 krad/s, then +20 dB/dec up to 10 krad/s, then flat again. This implies:
choices: A zero at 1 krad/s and a pole at 10 krad/s
A pole at 1 krad/s and a zero at 10 krad/s
items.explanation: The upward break (+20 dB/dec) at 1 krad/s is a zero. The slope returning to flat at 10 krad/s means a pole there cancels the zero's +20 dB/dec (pole adds -20). Net: zero at 1 krad/s, pole at 10 krad/s, giving a +20 dB shelf between them.

items.front: How do you read poles and zeros off a measured/asymptotic Bode magnitude plot?
items.back: Locate each frequency where the slope changes by a multiple of 20 dB/decade. A downward break (slope decreases by 20) is a pole at that corner; an upward break (slope increases by 20) is a zero. The number of poles minus zeros equals the final high-frequency rolloff slope divided by -20 dB/decade. Confirm with the phase plot.
items.hint: Slope breaks = corners; down = pole, up = zero; count multiples of 20 dB/dec.

items.front: What is the time constant of a series RC circuit, and what are its units?
items.back: tau = R*C, in seconds (ohms * farads = seconds). It sets the speed of charge/discharge: larger R or C means a slower response.
items.hint: Multiply the two element values.

items.front: What is the time constant of a series RL circuit?
items.back: tau = L/R, in seconds (henries / ohms = seconds). Note the inversion relative to RC: here larger R speeds the circuit up.
items.hint: Inductor over resistor.

items.front: State the universal exponential for a first-order step response from initial value Vi to final value Vf.
items.back: v(t) = Vf + (Vi - Vf)*exp(-t/tau). It applies to any first-order state variable (cap voltage or inductor current), whether charging or discharging.
items.hint: Final plus the gap, decaying.

items.front: What is the '5-tau rule'?
items.back: After 5 time constants the response has settled to within ~0.67% of its final value (exp(-5) = 0.0067), so the circuit is treated as fully settled. One tau reaches ~63.2%, and 3 tau reaches ~95%.
items.hint: How many tau to call it done.

items.front: How do you read tau directly off a scope trace of an RC charging curve?
items.back: tau is the time to reach 63.2% of the final value, OR the time for a discharge to fall to 36.8%. Equivalently, the initial tangent extrapolates to the final value at t = tau.
items.hint: 63% point, or the initial-slope intercept.

items.prompt: A 10 kohm resistor charges a 1 uF capacitor from a step source. Approximately how long until the cap is fully charged (within ~1%)?
items.explanation: tau = R*C = 10e3 * 1e-6 = 10 ms. The 5-tau rule gives 5 * 10 ms = 50 ms to settle within ~1%.

items.prompt: In an RL circuit, the inductor current after one time constant of a step input reaches approximately what fraction of its final value?
items.explanation: Any first-order rising exponential reaches 1 - exp(-1) = 0.632, i.e. 63.2%, after one tau. Inductor current is the state variable that rises like cap voltage does.

items.prompt: Which quantity cannot change instantaneously in an ideal inductor?
items.explanation: v = L*di/dt, so an instantaneous current change would require infinite voltage. Inductor current is a state variable and is continuous; its voltage can jump.

items.prompt: An RC network passes the high-frequency edges of a square wave but droops on the flat tops. The output is approximately the derivative of the input. This means tau is...
choices: Much longer than the pulse width
Much shorter than the pulse width
Exactly equal to the pulse width
items.explanation: When tau << pulse width, the RC high-pass differentiates: it responds only to fast changes (edges) and the capacitor fully charges during the flat top, producing the derivative-like spikes.

items.front: What is the undamped natural frequency w0 of a series or parallel RLC circuit?
items.back: w0 = 1/sqrt(L*C) in rad/s. It is the frequency at which the lossless circuit would oscillate and the center frequency of resonance.
items.hint: Depends only on L and C, not R.

items.front: Define the damping ratio zeta and what its value tells you.
items.back: zeta is the dimensionless ratio comparing actual damping to critical damping. zeta < 1 underdamped (rings), zeta = 1 critically damped (fastest with no overshoot), zeta > 1 overdamped (slow, no ring).
items.hint: Compares to the critical-damping boundary at 1.

items.front: Write the standard second-order characteristic equation in terms of w0 and zeta.
items.back: s^2 + 2*zeta*w0*s + w0^2 = 0. Its roots (poles) determine the transient: complex roots ring, real roots decay without oscillation.
items.hint: Coefficients are 1, 2*zeta*w0, w0^2.

items.front: For a SERIES RLC circuit, give zeta in terms of R, L, C.
items.back: zeta = (R/2)*sqrt(C/L). Equivalently the damping coefficient alpha = R/(2*L), and zeta = alpha/w0. More series R means more damping.
items.hint: R over 2, times sqrt(C/L).

items.front: How do you identify the damping regime of a step response by eye on a scope?
items.back: Overshoot with decaying oscillation = underdamped (zeta<1). A single fast rise with no overshoot, just barely no ring = critically damped (zeta=1). A slow sluggish rise with no overshoot = overdamped (zeta>1).
items.hint: Look for ringing vs. sluggishness.

items.prompt: A series RLC step response shows decaying sinusoidal ringing before settling. What is the damping regime?
items.explanation: Ringing (overshoot plus decaying oscillation) is the signature of underdamping, zeta < 1, where the characteristic-equation roots are complex conjugates with a negative real part.

items.prompt: Which condition gives the FASTEST settling with NO overshoot?
items.explanation: Critical damping (zeta = 1) is the boundary: it reaches final value as fast as possible without any overshoot. zeta < 1 overshoots; zeta > 1 is slower.

items.prompt: A series RLC has L = 100 uH and C = 100 pF. What is w0?
In an underdamped circuit, the ringing frequency (damped natural frequency wd) compared to w0 is...
items.explanation: wd = w0*sqrt(1 - zeta^2), which is always less than w0 for any damping (zeta > 0). The damping pulls the observed ring frequency below the undamped natural frequency.

items.front: At resonance, what happens to the impedance of a SERIES RLC versus a PARALLEL (tank) RLC?
items.back: Series RLC impedance is at a MINIMUM (just R, since XL and XC cancel) so current peaks. Parallel/tank RLC impedance is at a MAXIMUM so the circulating tank current is large but line current is small.
items.hint: Series minimum, tank maximum.

items.front: Define quality factor Q in terms of resonant frequency and bandwidth.
items.back: Q = f0/BW, so BW = f0/Q. Higher Q means a narrower, more selective response. Q also equals 2*pi times energy stored per cycle divided by energy dissipated.
items.hint: Ratio of center frequency to bandwidth.

items.front: Give Q for a SERIES RLC and for a PARALLEL RLC in terms of R, L, C.
items.back: Series: Q = (1/R)*sqrt(L/C) = w0*L/R. Parallel: Q = R*sqrt(C/L) = R/(w0*L). Note R helps Q in parallel but hurts Q in series.
items.hint: They are reciprocals in how R enters.

items.front: How is the bandwidth BW defined on a resonance curve, and what points mark it?
items.back: BW = f2 - f1, the span between the two half-power (-3 dB) frequencies where the response falls to 1/sqrt(2) (0.707) of peak. These are the points where stored vs. dissipated power balance shifts.
items.hint: The -3 dB / 0.707 points.

items.front: What is the relationship between damping ratio zeta and Q?
items.back: Q = 1/(2*zeta). High Q means low damping (sharp resonance, long ringing); critical damping (zeta=1) corresponds to Q = 0.5. This links the transient (ringing) and frequency (selectivity) views.
items.hint: Reciprocal of twice zeta.

items.prompt: A bandpass filter is centered at f0 = 10 MHz with Q = 50. What is its bandwidth?
items.explanation: BW = f0/Q = 10 MHz / 50 = 200 kHz. Higher Q would narrow this further, increasing selectivity.

items.prompt: You raise the Q of a tuned amplifier's tank circuit. What happens to selectivity and bandwidth?
items.explanation: Since BW = f0/Q, raising Q narrows the bandwidth, which sharpens the response and improves selectivity (better rejection of off-frequency signals).

items.prompt: A series RLC has R = 10 ohm, L = 1 mH, C = 1 nF. What is Q at resonance?
In an LC oscillator, a high-Q tank circuit is desirable primarily because it...
choices: Improves frequency stability and spectral purity
Lowers the supply current to zero
Eliminates the need for an active device
items.explanation: A high-Q tank rings cleanly at f0 with low loss, giving better frequency stability and a purer (narrower) spectrum. The active device still supplies energy to sustain oscillation.

items.prompt: For an antenna matching network, a higher loaded Q generally implies...
choices: Wider matched bandwidth, easier to use over a band
Narrower matched bandwidth, more sensitive to frequency
Lower insertion loss at all frequencies
items.explanation: Higher Q means narrower bandwidth (BW = f0/Q). A high-Q match is sharper and only good over a narrow frequency range, so it is more sensitive to detuning.

lessons.title: State Variables and the s-Domain View

items.front: What are the state variables of an RLC circuit and why?
items.back: Capacitor voltage and inductor current. They store energy and cannot change instantaneously, so they fully define the circuit's energy state; all initial conditions are expressed through them.
items.hint: The two energy-storing quantities that are continuous.

items.front: How do an inductor and capacitor transform into the s-domain (with zero initial conditions)?
items.back: Inductor impedance Z = s*L; capacitor impedance Z = 1/(s*C). The s-domain turns differential equations into algebra, so you solve transients with ordinary network analysis.
items.hint: s*L and 1/(s*C).

items.front: How do nonzero initial conditions appear in s-domain models?
items.back: A charged capacitor adds a series source (or current source IC = C*v0); an inductor with initial current adds a series voltage source (or parallel source IL = i0/s). Initial energy enters as extra sources.
items.hint: Initial cap voltage and inductor current become added sources.

items.front: How do the locations of the s-plane poles relate to the transient response?
items.back: Real negative poles = exponential decay (over/critically damped). Complex-conjugate poles = damped oscillation; the imaginary part sets ring frequency wd, the real part sets the decay rate (envelope). Poles on the imaginary axis = undamped oscillation.
items.hint: Real part = decay, imaginary part = ring.

items.front: Why does solving an RLC transient by differential equations and by Laplace give the same answer?
items.back: The Laplace transform encodes the same describing differential equation; algebraic solution in s plus inverse transform reproduces the time-domain solution. Laplace just folds the initial conditions and convolution into algebra.
items.hint: Same ODE, different bookkeeping.

items.prompt: In the s-domain, a capacitor C with zero initial charge is represented by an impedance of...
items.explanation: Capacitor impedance is 1/(s*C). As s (frequency) rises the impedance falls, consistent with a cap passing high frequencies.

items.prompt: Complex-conjugate pole pairs at s = -alpha +/- j*wd correspond to what time-domain behavior?
items.explanation: The negative real part -alpha gives the decaying envelope exp(-alpha*t); the imaginary part wd gives the oscillation. Together: an exponentially decaying sinusoid (ringing).

items.prompt: If a circuit's poles lie exactly on the imaginary (jw) axis, the natural response is...
items.explanation: Poles on the jw axis have zero real part, so there is no decay (zeta = 0): the response is a sustained sinusoidal oscillation at that frequency, the ideal lossless LC case.

items.prompt: Just after a switch closes in an RLC circuit, the inductor initially behaves like a/an...
choices: Open circuit (it holds its prior current)
Ideal voltage source equal to the supply
items.explanation: Inductor current is a state variable and cannot jump; if it was zero it stays zero just after switching, so the inductor momentarily acts like an open circuit (no current). A capacitor, by contrast, initially acts like a short if uncharged.

items.front: What does doping do to a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor?
items.back: Doping adds impurity atoms to set the dominant carrier type. Donors (e.g. phosphorus, group V) create n-type material with excess free electrons; acceptors (e.g. boron, group III) create p-type material with excess holes. This raises conductivity far above intrinsic silicon and is controllable over many orders of magnitude.
items.hint: Group V vs group III impurities.

items.front: What is the depletion region of a PN junction?
items.back: At the metallurgical junction, electrons and holes diffuse across and recombine, leaving behind ionized dopant atoms. This forms a region depleted of free carriers with a built-in electric field (and built-in potential ~0.6-0.7V for Si). The field opposes further diffusion and sets up the junction's barrier.
items.hint: Carriers diffuse, leaving fixed charge behind.

items.front: Contrast forward bias and reverse bias of a diode.
items.back: Forward bias (anode positive w.r.t. cathode) shrinks the depletion region and lowers the barrier, allowing large current once V exceeds the turn-on knee. Reverse bias (cathode positive) widens the depletion region, blocks conduction, and passes only a tiny saturation/leakage current Is until breakdown.
items.hint: Which polarity narrows the depletion region?

items.front: State the Shockley diode equation and define its terms.
items.back: I = Is*(e^(V/(n*Vt)) - 1). Is is the reverse saturation current, V the voltage across the junction, n the ideality factor (~1-2), and Vt the thermal voltage = kT/q (~25.85 mV at 300 K, often rounded to ~26 mV). The exponential dominates in forward bias; in strong reverse bias I approaches -Is.
items.hint: Exponential of V over n times Vt, minus one.

items.prompt: Approximately what is the thermal voltage Vt = kT/q at room temperature (~300 K)?
items.explanation: Vt = kT/q is about 25.85 mV near 300 K, conventionally rounded to ~26 mV (or 25 mV). It is not the 0.7V Si forward drop, which is a different quantity arising from the exponential I-V relation.

items.prompt: Typical forward voltage drops: which set is correct for Si, Ge, and Schottky diodes?
items.explanation: Silicon PN junctions conduct at ~0.7V, germanium at ~0.3V, and Schottky (metal-semiconductor) at ~0.2-0.4V. The lower Schottky drop and fast switching make it attractive for rectifiers and detectors.

items.prompt: In strong reverse bias (and below breakdown), the Shockley equation predicts the current approaches:
items.explanation: For V negative and |V| >> n*Vt, e^(V/(n*Vt)) -> 0, so I -> Is*(0 - 1) = -Is. The current saturates at a small reverse leakage of magnitude Is until reverse breakdown occurs.

items.front: Why does the silicon diode forward drop have a negative temperature coefficient (~ -2 mV/C)?
items.back: Is rises strongly with temperature (roughly doubling every ~10C), so for a fixed forward current the required V falls by about 2 mV per degree C. This is exploited in temperature sensors and bandgap references, and matters for biasing because a diode/transistor junction conducts more at the same V as it heats.
items.hint: Is grows with T, so V at fixed I must fall.

items.front: What is the small-signal dynamic resistance r_d of a forward-biased diode?
items.back: r_d = n*Vt / I, the slope inverse of the I-V curve at the operating point. At room temperature with n=1, r_d ~ 26 mV / I (e.g. ~26 ohm at 1 mA, ~2.6 ohm at 10 mA). It models the diode for small AC signals riding on a DC bias, as in detectors and mixers.
items.hint: Differentiate the diode equation; r_d = dV/dI.

items.front: List the common piecewise diode models from simplest to most detailed.
items.back: 1) Ideal: short when forward, open when reverse (0V drop). 2) Constant-drop: fixed ~0.7V when conducting, open otherwise. 3) Piecewise-linear: constant drop plus a series resistance r_s for the I-V slope. 4) Small-signal: the operating-point model using r_d = n*Vt/I for AC analysis.
items.hint: Ideal -> constant drop -> add series R -> AC r_d.

items.prompt: For a half-wave rectifier driven by a sine of peak Vp (using the constant-drop model with one Si diode), the peak output across the load is about:
items.explanation: A half-wave rectifier conducts through one diode on positive half-cycles, so the peak output is Vp minus one diode drop (~0.7V). The bridge subtracts two drops (~1.4V) because two diodes conduct in series.

items.prompt: A full-wave BRIDGE rectifier compared with a full-wave CENTER-TAPPED rectifier:
choices: Uses 1 diode vs 2 and needs no transformer
Uses 4 diodes with two drops per cycle but no center tap; center-tapped uses 2 diodes (one drop) but needs a tapped secondary
Cannot be filtered with a capacitor
items.explanation: The bridge uses 4 diodes (two conduct in series each half-cycle, ~1.4V total drop) and a simple secondary. The center-tapped version uses 2 diodes (one drop ~0.7V) but requires a center-tapped winding and each diode sees higher PIV (~2*Vp).

items.front: What is Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) and roughly what value must the diodes survive in (a) a bridge and (b) a center-tapped full-wave rectifier?
items.back: PIV is the maximum reverse voltage a diode must block. In a bridge, each diode sees about Vp (one secondary peak, minus a drop). In a center-tapped rectifier, the off diode sees the full winding peak-to-peak, roughly 2*Vp. With a filter cap charged near Vp, derate generously above these figures.
items.hint: Center-tapped stresses the off diode with ~2x the bridge.

items.front: Capacitor-input filter: give the ripple formula and the cap-sizing rearrangement.
items.back: Approximate ripple Vr ~ I_load / (f * C), where f is the ripple frequency (line freq for half-wave, 2*line for full-wave). Rearranged to size the cap: C ~ I_load / (f * Vr). Larger C and higher f reduce ripple; full-wave halves the discharge time vs half-wave, so it needs about half the capacitance for the same ripple.
items.hint: Q = C*V and I = C*dV/dt over the discharge interval.

items.prompt: A full-wave bridge on a 60 Hz line drives 0.5 A and you want <= 1V peak-to-peak ripple. Using Vr ~ I/(f*C) with f=120 Hz, the filter cap is about:
items.explanation: C ~ I/(f*Vr) = 0.5 / (120 * 1) = 0.00417 F = ~4170 uF. Note f=120 Hz because a full-wave rectifier produces two output pulses per line cycle. A half-wave version (f=60 Hz) would need roughly double.

items.prompt: Why does a larger filter capacitor cause higher peak diode/transformer currents even though average load current is unchanged?
choices: It does not; current is constant
The cap recharges only during a brief interval near the peak, so charge must be delivered in short high-amplitude current pulses
Larger caps lower the line frequency
It raises the diode forward voltage
items.explanation: With a stiff cap the output stays near Vp and the diodes conduct only in a narrow window near each peak. All the charge delivered to the load over the cycle must be replenished in that short time, producing tall, narrow current spikes that stress the diodes and transformer.

items.front: In a power-supply front end, why are Schottky diodes often chosen for the output rectifier of a switching converter?
items.back: Schottky diodes have a low forward drop (~0.3V) that cuts conduction loss, and they are majority-carrier devices with essentially no reverse-recovery charge, so they switch cleanly at high frequency. Tradeoffs: higher reverse leakage, lower reverse voltage ratings, and worse leakage at high temperature versus silicon PN diodes.
items.hint: Low Vf and fast switching vs limited reverse rating.

items.prompt: The piecewise-linear model adds what to the constant-drop model to better fit the measured forward I-V curve?
choices: A series resistance r_s representing the curve's finite slope
items.explanation: Piecewise-linear approximates conduction as a fixed offset (~0.7V) in series with a resistance r_s, giving a sloped line instead of a vertical one. This captures the additional drop at high current better than the flat constant-drop model.

items.front: What happens at reverse breakdown, and how does a Zener diode use it?
items.back: Beyond the reverse breakdown voltage, current rises sharply (via Zener tunneling below ~5V and avalanche above ~6V) while the voltage stays nearly constant. A Zener diode is designed to operate in this region as a voltage reference or shunt regulator, holding Vz across itself as long as it is kept within its current limits.
items.hint: Sharp knee where V is nearly fixed.

items.front: Sketch the basic Zener shunt regulator and its design current relation.
items.back: A series resistor Rs feeds a node where the Zener (cathode to +) shunts to ground and the load taps across it. I_Rs = (Vin - Vz)/Rs splits into I_zener + I_load. Rs must be chosen so the Zener stays above its minimum current at max load / min Vin, and below its power limit at min load / max Vin.
items.hint: Series R sets total current; Zener absorbs the slack.

items.prompt: A Zener shunt regulator stops regulating (output sags below Vz) when:
choices: Load current rises so high that little or no current is left for the Zener, dropping it out of breakdown
Vin exceeds Vz by too much
items.explanation: If I_load approaches (Vin - Vz)/Rs, the Zener current falls below its minimum and it leaves the breakdown region, so the output drops with load. The other classic failure is the opposite extreme: at light load and high Vin the Zener can exceed its power rating and overheat.

items.prompt: Which is a fundamental limitation of a simple Zener shunt regulator versus a series-pass linear regulator?
choices: It cannot produce a stable voltage
Poor efficiency and limited load range, since Rs and the Zener must waste current; load and line regulation are set by Zener impedance Rz
items.explanation: The shunt regulator continuously dissipates current in Rs and the Zener regardless of load, so it is inefficient and tolerates only a limited load range. Output stiffness is limited by the Zener's dynamic impedance Rz; precision designs buffer it or use a dedicated reference IC.

items.front: What is a diode clipper (limiter), and give a protection use case.
items.back: A clipper limits a signal's excursion by conducting once it exceeds a threshold, clamping the peak to about that level. Two diodes to the rails (or back-to-back at an input) protect an ADC, op-amp, or logic input by shunting overvoltage transients. This is the diode-clamp input protection familiar from instrumentation and radar receiver front-ends.
items.hint: Diode conducts and holds the peak at a set level.

items.prompt: A diode CLAMP (DC restorer) differs from a CLIPPER in that it:
choices: Shifts the entire waveform's DC level so a chosen point sits at a reference, without changing its shape
items.explanation: A clamp uses a series capacitor plus a diode (and optional bias) to add a DC offset so the waveform's peak (or valley) rests at a reference level, preserving the AC shape. A clipper instead chops off part of the waveform that exceeds a threshold.

items.front: How does a half-wave (peak) voltage doubler work?
items.back: A clamp stage (series cap + diode) shifts the input up by ~Vp, then a peak-rectifier stage (diode + cap) captures the ~2*Vp peak. Cascading clamp/peak stages yields triplers/quadruplers (Cockcroft-Walton multiplier). Useful for generating high DC voltages from a modest AC source, at the cost of poor regulation and high output impedance.
items.hint: Clamp then peak-detect; stack stages for higher multiples.

items.prompt: Why are back-to-back diodes (or a TVS/clamp) placed at a sensitive analog input rather than relying on the device's internal protection?
choices: To shunt ESD and overvoltage transients away before they reach the input, holding the pin within a safe range
items.explanation: External clamp diodes (to the rails or a TVS) divert transient energy and limit the pin voltage to roughly a diode drop beyond the rails, protecting the input stage. Internal clamps exist but have limited current handling, so external protection is added for robustness in harsh environments.

items.front: Why must a Zener (or clamp) diode's dynamic impedance Rz be considered, not just its nominal Vz?
items.back: Rz is the slope of the breakdown region; the regulated voltage actually moves by delta_I * Rz as current changes, degrading load and line regulation. It also injects noise. Lower-impedance Zeners regulate better, and references near 5-6V have the lowest temperature coefficient because Zener and avalanche tempcos cancel.
items.hint: V across it is not perfectly flat; slope = Rz.

items.front: What is a varactor (varicap) diode and what is it used for?
items.back: A varactor is a reverse-biased diode used as a voltage-controlled capacitor: the depletion width (and thus junction capacitance) varies with reverse voltage. It tunes VCOs, PLLs, RF filters, and electronic tuning circuits, replacing mechanical tuning. Hyperabrupt-junction types give a wider, more linear C-vs-V tuning range.
items.hint: Reverse-bias varies depletion capacitance for tuning.

items.prompt: A varactor diode is operated in which bias condition?
items.explanation: A varactor is used reverse-biased (below breakdown), where increasing reverse voltage widens the depletion region and reduces junction capacitance. This C-vs-V relationship provides the voltage-controlled tuning element for VCOs and tuned circuits.

items.front: How does a photodiode operate, and in what two modes?
items.back: Light absorbed in or near the junction generates electron-hole pairs that produce a current. Photovoltaic mode (zero bias) gives low noise and is used in solar cells / precision light sensing; photoconductive mode (reverse bias) widens the depletion region for faster response and higher bandwidth, used in optical receivers. A PIN photodiode adds an intrinsic layer for speed and efficiency.
items.hint: Zero-bias (photovoltaic) vs reverse-bias (photoconductive).

items.prompt: What primarily distinguishes a PIN diode from an ordinary PN diode in RF use?
choices: An intrinsic (undoped) layer between P and N makes it act as a current-controlled RF resistor / switch and attenuator at high frequency
items.explanation: The wide intrinsic region stores charge so that at RF the PIN diode behaves as a variable resistance set by its DC forward (bias) current, enabling RF switches, attenuators, and phase shifters. It does not rectify the RF the way a small junction would.

items.front: Why is a Schottky diode the preferred detector/mixer element in RF receivers and radar?
items.back: As a majority-carrier device it has negligible minority-carrier storage and very fast response, plus a low turn-on (~0.2-0.4V) giving good detection sensitivity. Low junction capacitance and low series resistance let it work at microwave frequencies as an envelope detector, mixer, or power detector.
items.hint: No stored charge, low Vf, fast at microwave.

items.prompt: A tunnel (Esaki) diode is notable in microwave circuits because it exhibits:
choices: A negative-resistance region in its forward I-V curve, usable for oscillators and amplifiers
items.explanation: Heavy doping makes a tunnel diode conduct by quantum tunneling at low forward voltage, then current DECREASES with increasing voltage over a range, giving negative differential resistance. That property allows high-frequency oscillators and amplifiers.

items.prompt: A Gunn diode generates microwave power based on:
choices: A PN junction in forward bias
The transferred-electron (negative differential mobility) effect in bulk n-GaAs, not a junction at all
items.explanation: A Gunn diode is not a junction diode; it relies on the transferred-electron effect in bulk III-V material (e.g. GaAs), where electrons move to a lower-mobility valley, producing negative differential resistance and microwave oscillation in a resonant cavity.

items.front: What determines an LED's forward voltage, and why does it differ from a silicon rectifier?
items.back: An LED's forward drop is set by the semiconductor bandgap of its emitted color: ~1.8-2.2V for red, up to ~3-3.5V for blue/white, versus ~0.7V for a silicon rectifier. Recombination across the wider bandgap emits a photon, so higher-energy (bluer) light requires a higher forward voltage. LEDs are driven with a current-limiting resistor or current source.
items.hint: Drop tracks the bandgap / photon energy (color).

items.prompt: When choosing between a Schottky and a silicon PN diode for a given role, the main Schottky drawback to weigh against its low drop and speed is:
choices: Higher reverse leakage and lower reverse-voltage ratings (worsening at high temperature)
items.explanation: Schottky diodes trade their low forward drop and fast switching for higher reverse leakage and generally lower reverse breakdown voltage, both of which worsen with temperature. Where high reverse voltage blocking or very low leakage is needed, a silicon PN diode is preferred.

items.front: What are the three terminals of a BJT and the layer each connects to?
items.back: Emitter (heavily doped), Base (thin, lightly doped), and Collector (moderately doped, large area). An NPN is n-p-n stacked; a PNP is p-n-p.
items.hint: Think E, B, C and which one is thin and lightly doped.

items.front: For an NPN in the forward-active region, how are the two junctions biased?
items.back: Base-emitter junction forward biased (about 0.7 V), base-collector junction reverse biased. This is the normal amplifying region.
items.hint: One junction conducts, one blocks.

items.front: Why is the base made thin and lightly doped?
items.back: So that most carriers injected from the emitter diffuse across the base to the collector instead of recombining, giving high beta. A thin base reduces recombination and transit time.
items.hint: It determines what fraction of emitter current reaches the collector.

items.front: Define cutoff, active, and saturation regions of a BJT.
items.back: Cutoff: both junctions reverse biased (or BE below ~0.6 V), transistor off. Active: BE forward, BC reverse, I_C = beta*I_B (amplifier). Saturation: both junctions forward biased, V_CE small (~0.2 V), I_C limited by external circuit (switch on).
items.hint: Defined by the bias state of the two junctions.

items.prompt: In an NPN BJT operating in the forward-active region, the base-collector junction is:
items.explanation: Forward-active requires BE forward biased and BC reverse biased. If BC also becomes forward biased the device enters saturation.

items.prompt: A BJT switch is fully ON (saturated). The collector-emitter voltage V_CE(sat) is approximately:
items.explanation: In saturation V_CE drops to roughly 0.1-0.3 V, making the device a good closed switch. The remaining drop dissipates little power because I_C is set by the load.

items.prompt: Which doping arrangement describes a PNP transistor?
items.explanation: A PNP is p-type emitter, n-type base, p-type collector. Conventional current and supply polarities are reversed relative to an NPN.

items.prompt: To use a BJT as a logic switch driving a relay, you typically operate it between which two regions?
items.explanation: As a switch the transistor toggles between cutoff (open) and saturation (closed). The active region is used for linear amplification, not switching.

items.front: What is the approximate base-emitter turn-on voltage for a silicon BJT, and how does it vary with temperature?
items.back: About 0.6 to 0.7 V. V_BE decreases roughly 2 mV per degree C rise at fixed I_C, which is a key cause of bias drift and thermal runaway.
items.hint: Diode-like junction with a negative tempco.

lessons.title: Current Gain, Early Effect, and DC Analysis

items.front: Define beta (hFE) and write the active-region current relations.
items.back: beta = I_C / I_B is the DC current gain. I_C = beta*I_B and I_E = I_C + I_B = (beta+1)*I_B. Typical beta is 50 to 300 and varies widely between units.
items.hint: KCL at the transistor: emitter carries both base and collector current.

items.front: Define alpha and relate it to beta.
items.back: alpha = I_C / I_E, the fraction of emitter current reaching the collector (typically 0.98 to 0.998). alpha = beta/(beta+1) and beta = alpha/(1-alpha).
items.hint: alpha is just below 1; beta is large.

items.front: What is the Early effect and how is it modeled?
items.back: As V_CE increases the reverse-biased BC depletion region widens, narrowing the effective base, so I_C rises slightly with V_CE. Modeled by the Early voltage V_A and output resistance r_o = V_A / I_C.
items.hint: It gives the active-region I-V curves a finite, nonzero slope.

items.front: What is a Q-point (quiescent point) and the load line?
items.back: The Q-point is the DC operating point (I_C, V_CE) with no signal applied. The DC load line is the set of (I_C, V_CE) allowed by V_CC and R_C: V_CE = V_CC - I_C*R_C. The Q-point sits where the load line crosses the chosen I_B curve.
items.hint: Static operating point set by biasing; one line set by the external resistors.

items.prompt: A BJT in active region has I_B = 20 uA and beta = 150. The collector current I_C is approximately:
items.explanation: I_C = beta*I_B = 150 * 20 uA = 3000 uA = 3 mA.

items.prompt: A transistor has Early voltage V_A = 100 V and operates at I_C = 1 mA. Its small-signal output resistance r_o is about:
items.explanation: r_o = V_A / I_C = 100 V / 1 mA = 100 kohm. Higher V_A or lower I_C gives larger r_o and a flatter output characteristic.

items.prompt: If alpha = 0.99, the corresponding beta is approximately:
items.explanation: beta = alpha/(1-alpha) = 0.99/0.01 = 99. Small changes in alpha near 1 produce large changes in beta.

items.prompt: For a common-emitter stage with V_CC = 10 V and R_C = 2 kohm, the saturation (bottom) end of the DC load line corresponds to I_C of about:
items.explanation: At saturation V_CE is near 0, so I_C(max) = V_CC/R_C = 10 V / 2 kohm = 5 mA. The cutoff end is I_C = 0 at V_CE = V_CC.

items.prompt: Why is the relation I_C = beta*I_B unreliable as the basis for setting a Q-point in production?
choices: Beta is exactly the same for all units of a part number
Beta varies 2-3x unit-to-unit and with temperature
items.explanation: Beta spreads widely (often 2-3x) across units and drifts with temperature, so designs that depend directly on beta have unstable operating points. Emitter degeneration and divider bias reduce this sensitivity.

items.front: In active region, how does I_C depend on V_BE?
items.back: Exponentially: I_C = I_S * exp(V_BE / Vt), where Vt is the thermal voltage (~26 mV at room temperature). A ~60 mV increase in V_BE multiplies I_C by about 10.
items.hint: Same exponential law as a diode.

lessons.title: Biasing for a Stable Q-Point and Small-Signal Models

items.front: Why is voltage-divider bias with an emitter resistor the standard biasing scheme?
items.back: The divider fixes the base voltage nearly independent of beta; the emitter resistor R_E sets I_E ~ (V_B - V_BE)/R_E via negative feedback. The Q-point becomes largely insensitive to beta spread and V_BE temperature drift.
items.hint: Stiff base voltage plus emitter degeneration.

items.front: Why is fixed (base-resistor) bias a poor choice for a stable Q-point?
items.back: It sets I_B from V_CC and R_B, so I_C = beta*I_B depends directly on beta. With beta varying 2-3x and drifting with temperature, the Q-point moves a lot and can drift toward saturation or cutoff.
items.hint: I_C tracks beta one-for-one.

items.front: How does emitter degeneration (R_E) stabilize the Q-point?
items.back: If I_C rises, the drop across R_E raises the emitter voltage, which reduces V_BE, which reduces I_C. This local negative feedback fights changes from beta spread and temperature, holding I_C nearly constant.
items.hint: Self-correcting feedback through the emitter.

items.front: Define the key small-signal parameters gm, r_pi, and r_o.
items.back: gm = I_C / Vt (transconductance). r_pi = beta / gm = beta*Vt / I_C (input resistance looking into the base). r_o = V_A / I_C (output resistance from the Early effect).
items.hint: All are set by the DC bias current I_C.

items.front: Contrast the hybrid-pi and T-models.
